md: A **function** is a rule that takes an input and returns *exactly one* output. Every function has a **domain** (the set of valid inputs) and a **range** (the set of all outputs the function actually produces). Understanding both is the price of admission to most of pre-calculus and calculus.

blocks.title: Plain English
blocks.body:
**Domain**: 'What values of $x$ am I *allowed* to plug in?'
**Range**: 'What values of $y$ can ever come out?'

md: Most functions are 'all real numbers' for the domain — *except* when the formula breaks. Three things break:
**Division by zero.** Any value that makes the denominator zero is excluded.
Find every value that triggers one of these — those are excluded. Everything else is in the domain.
Range is usually trickier than domain. Think about *what y-values* the graph hits. A picture often makes this obvious:

The widget shows $y = x^2$. **Domain**: all real numbers (you can square any number). **Range**: $[0, \infty)$ — the parabola never goes below zero. Drag the sliders to see how range changes — flip $a$ negative and the range becomes $(-\infty, 0]$ instead.

**$f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x - 3}$.** Denominator is zero when $x = 3$. So **domain** is $\{x : x \neq 3\}$. **Range**: every real except $0$ (the function never outputs zero). Domain $= \mathbb{R} \setminus \{3\}$, range $= \mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}$.
**$g(x) = \sqrt{x - 4}$.** Need $x - 4 \geq 0$, so $x \geq 4$. Domain: $[4, \infty)$. Square roots are non-negative, so range: $[0, \infty)$.
**$h(x) = \sin x$.** No restrictions, domain is $\mathbb{R}$. Output is always between $-1$ and $1$, so range is $[-1, 1]$.

blocks.title: Interval notation
blocks.body: $[a, b]$ — both endpoints included. $(a, b)$ — neither included. Mixed: $[a, b)$ or $(a, b]$. $\infty$ always gets a parenthesis since you can't 'reach' infinity. Use $\cup$ for unions: e.g., $\mathbb{R} \setminus \{3\}$ can be written $(-\infty, 3) \cup (3, \infty)$.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Domain** = allowed inputs, **range** = possible outputs. Find domain by ruling out divide-by-zero, square root of negative, and log of non-positive. Range usually requires reasoning about the function's graph or behavior.
